headlines.geopolitics: Gas prices surge to $3.98 nationally as Iran conflict disrupts energy supplies
headlines.economy: Seoul shantytown resident faces eviction from $2.6M Gangnam district for $3,300
headlines.domestic: Two pilots killed in first deadly LaGuardia crash in 30 years during foggy conditions

clips.caption: Duke explains why his wife controls the gas budget now
Jax's dating app disaster gets worse
Pringle discovers what a thirst trap is

script.text: I accidentally super-liked my Uber driver on Hinge and now he knows where I live.
script.direction: mortified confession, slightly breathless

script.text: Gas prices have hit three dollars and ninety-eight cents per gallon nationally, up a full dollar in just one month as the Iran conflict continues to disrupt global energy supplies.
script.direction: authoritative news delivery, measured pace

script.text: The surge is hitting hardest on the West Coast, where California drivers are now paying five dollars and eighty-seven cents per gallon, while diesel has climbed even faster to five forty-one nationwide.
script.direction: building urgency, emphasis on specific numbers

script.text: That diesel number matters because it drives freight costs, which means these price increases will ripple through supply chains and hit consumers across the broader economy.
script.direction: connecting dots for audience, slightly slower on key phrase

script.text: This marks the first time diesel has topped five dollars since December twenty twenty-two, and analysts warn we could see sustained higher prices as long as the Middle East situation remains unstable.
script.direction: grave implications, pause after key statistic

script.text: In Seoul, South Korea, eighty-five-year-old Lee Chang-wol is facing eviction from a shantytown in Gangnam, the city's wealthiest district, after rejecting a government buyout offer of thirty-three hundred dollars for her one-room hut.
script.direction: human interest tone, emphasizing the contrast

script.text: She's lived in Guryong Village for nearly four decades without even a toilet, while typical three-bedroom apartments just blocks away cost two point six million dollars.
script.direction: incredulous at the disparity, building to the number

script.text: The city has told her to vacate by mid-June or face forced eviction as part of a broader redevelopment plan for the area.
script.direction: matter-of-fact delivery of harsh reality

script.text: Here at home, LaGuardia Airport experienced its first deadly crash in more than three decades Sunday night when an Air Canada flight collided with a Port Authority fire truck during rainy, foggy conditions.
script.direction: serious, breaking news tone

script.text: Two Canadian pilots were killed and forty-one others injured, but remarkably, no passengers died despite what investigators are calling a high-speed collision of heavy machinery.
script.direction: balancing tragedy with relief, emphasis on 'remarkably'

script.text: A flight attendant was thrown more than three hundred feet from the wreckage but survived, and by this week, only five people remained hospitalized.
script.direction: amazement at survival, specific on the distance

script.text: The fire truck lacked a tracking device that's standard at many airports, raising questions about emergency vehicle protocols during low-visibility conditions.
script.direction: investigative follow-up, identifying key safety gap

script.text: Professor, these gas prices are bringing back memories of the nineteen seventies. What should we understand about how energy crises reshape American behavior?
script.direction: setting up historical context, deferential to Pringle

script.text: The nineteen seventy-three oil embargo, Leo, was the moment Americans discovered they were hostage to events they couldn't control in places they couldn't pronounce.
script.direction: professorial authority, building to a point

script.text: Before October sixth, nineteen seventy-three, gasoline was thirty-eight cents a gallon and Americans drove wherever they pleased without thinking twice about it.
script.direction: nostalgic setup, emphasizing the innocence

script.text: Then Egypt and Syria attacked Israel on Yom Kippur, the United States resupplied Israel, and suddenly Arab oil ministers were dictating American domestic policy from Riyadh.
script.direction: building narrative tension, connecting distant events to home

script.text: Prices quadrupled overnight. Cars lined up for blocks at gas stations. The federal government imposed a fifty-five mile per hour speed limit and daylight saving time year-round.
script.direction: staccato delivery of escalating consequences

script.text: And that changed American car culture permanently, didn't it?
script.direction: following the thread, encouraging Pringle to continue

script.text: Completely. Detroit had been building land yachts that got eight miles per gallon. Suddenly Americans wanted Honda Civics and Toyota Corollas.
script.direction: relishing the irony, emphasis on the foreign names

script.text: More importantly, it created the modern environmental movement and the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, both direct responses to that vulnerability.
script.direction: connecting to lasting policy changes

script.text: Are we seeing that same psychological shift now with these Iran-related price spikes?
script.direction: pressing for contemporary relevance

script.text: The difference is we've been here before. Americans in twenty twenty-six know energy prices can spike. They adjust their behavior accordingly.
script.direction: distinguishing this crisis from the first

script.text: In nineteen seventy-three, it was genuinely shocking. Today it's genuinely expensive, but not shocking. That's the lesson of experience.
script.direction: measured conclusion, emphasizing the key distinction

script.text: That's your Download. Now let's talk about what all of this actually means for real people.
script.direction: transitional, warming up slightly

script.text: Jax, Duke, you've been listening.
script.direction: bringing in the other voices, casual invitation

script.text: Oh we've been listening alright. I just calculated how much it's gonna cost to drive to my parents' house next month and I think I'm gonna start walking.
script.direction: mock despair, exaggerated for effect

script.text: Jenny took over our gas budget last week. She handed me a card and said I get forty dollars. I said forty dollars for what? She said per week, Deepak.
script.direction: deadpan delivery, slight emphasis on his real name

script.text: Your wife gave you an allowance?
script.direction: incredulous amusement

script.text: My wife gave me a reality check. Apparently I was spending two hundred a week on gas and didn't even notice.
script.direction: sheepish admission

script.text: Two hundred dollars a week? Good heavens, what were you driving, a Sherman tank?
script.direction: genuine shock, historical reference comes naturally

script.text: A Ford F-one-fifty that I definitely do not need for my suburban lifestyle, but it makes me feel like I could haul something if I had to.
script.direction: self-aware about his own choices

script.text: The LaGuardia crash, though. I covered aviation for years and that survival rate is remarkable given the conditions.
script.direction: shifting to more serious topic, professional perspective

script.text: Fog is terrifying for anything with moving parts. We lost more people to training accidents in fog than we did to actual combat in some rotations.
script.direction: matter-of-fact, drawing from specific experience

script.text: Wait, the fire truck didn't have GPS? In twenty twenty-six? My DoorDash driver has better tracking than emergency vehicles?
script.direction: building outrage over the absurdity

script.text: That's exactly what investigators are asking. Standard technology, somehow not implemented.
script.direction: confirming Jax's instinct is correct

script.text: Speaking of tracking, Jenny can see exactly where I am at all times through Find My Friends, and last Tuesday she texted me 'why are you at Target for two hours?'
script.direction: setting up confession, slightly embarrassed

script.text: Oh no. What were you actually doing?
script.direction: leaning in, sensing a good story

script.text: I was sitting in my truck in the parking lot having a panic attack about gas prices and calling my mom to complain about Jenny controlling the budget.
script.direction: mortified confession, getting it all out

script.text: So I told her I was comparison shopping for toilet paper, which was somehow even more embarrassing than the truth.
script.direction: realizing he made it worse

script.text: You called your mother to complain about your wife?
script.direction: scandalized by the breach of protocol

script.text: My mother immediately took Jenny's side and said I'm lucky she doesn't control the whole checking account. Then she hung up on me.
script.direction: defeated, realizing everyone was against him

script.text: Your mom roasted you and hung up? That's brutal.
script.direction: sympathetic but also finding it funny

script.text: I'm trying not to laugh but that's exactly what Mia would do if I called her to complain about her mother.
script.direction: trying to be supportive but clearly amused

script.text: Speaking of technology disasters, I have to tell you guys about my dating app situation because it's gotten so much worse since we last talked.
script.direction: transitioning to his own humiliation, building anticipation

script.text: Oh no. What happened now?
script.direction: bracing for impact, genuinely concerned for his friend

script.text: So you know how I've been using multiple apps, right? Hinge, Bumble, the works. Well last week I'm ordering an Uber to go meet this girl from Hinge.
script.direction: setting up the story, building to the disaster

script.text: The driver shows up, he's this cute guy about my age, and I'm scrolling through Hinge while we're stuck in traffic, you know, multitasking.
script.direction: casual delivery, not yet realizing the problem

script.text: I see this profile that looks interesting, swipe right, and immediately get the notification that it's a match. Great, right?
script.direction: false enthusiasm, setting up the punchline

script.text: Please tell me you didn't—
script.direction: seeing where this is going, trying to interrupt

script.text: I matched with my Uber driver. Who was currently driving me to a date with someone else. And he could see his phone lighting up with notifications in the front seat.
script.direction: mortified, getting it all out in a rush

script.text: Oh man. What did you do?
script.direction: secondhand embarrassment, fascinated

script.text: I panicked and tried to unmatch him immediately, but you know how the app works, right? It sends a notification that says 'Jax super-liked you!'
script.direction: horror building, realizing it got worse

script.text: Except I didn't super-like him. I just regular liked him. But I was frantically trying to undo it and I guess I hit the wrong button.
script.direction: explaining the technical disaster

script.text: I'm going to need someone to explain what a super-like is and why it's worse than a regular like.
script.direction: genuinely confused, asking for clarification

script.text: A super-like is like, aggressively interested. It's saying 'I'm really into you specifically.' So now this guy thinks I'm hitting on him while he's driving me to another date.
script.direction: explaining while reliving the horror

script.text: How did the rest of the ride go?
script.direction: morbid curiosity

script.text: He turned around at the next red light and said 'Thanks for the super-like, but I'm seeing someone.' And I just said 'That was an accident' which somehow made it worse.
script.direction: dying inside, recounting the awkwardness

script.text: You told him it was an accident? That's like telling someone their baby is ugly by mistake.
script.direction: pointing out how he made it worse

script.text: And then I had to sit in silence for fifteen more minutes while he drove me to meet someone I wasn't even that excited about anymore because I was so mortified.
script.direction: the continuing humiliation

script.text: How was the actual date?
script.direction: almost afraid to ask

script.text: Terrible. I was so flustered I kept checking my phone to see if the Uber driver had unmatched me yet. She asked if I was expecting an important call and I said 'Yeah, from my Uber driver.'
script.direction: realizing how insane he sounded

script.text: This is why Peggy and I met at a library book sale. You argued about Dickens for two hours and then exchanged telephone numbers like civilized people.
script.direction: nostalgic for simpler times, slightly condescending

script.text: The worst part is he still hasn't unmatched me. Every day I see his profile and remember that he knows where I live and thinks I'm desperately attracted to him.
script.direction: ongoing torture, building to crescendo

script.text: Have you considered just using a different ride service?
script.direction: practical suggestion, missing the point

script.text: I've been taking the bus for two weeks. The bus! In Nashville! Do you know how long it takes to get anywhere on the bus in Nashville?
script.direction: outraged at his reduced circumstances

script.text: You're avoiding Uber because you're embarrassed about one driver?
script.direction: incredulous at the lengths Jax is going

script.text: What if I get him again? What if he tells other drivers? What if there's a group chat?
script.direction: spiraling into paranoid scenarios

script.text: Is there some sort of modern equivalent to changing your name and moving to another town? Because that seems to be what you're looking for.
script.direction: dry observation, genuinely trying to help

script.text: I have to ask — did you ever hear back from the girl you were supposed to meet that night?
script.direction: following up on the original date

script.text: She texted me the next day and said I seemed 'distracted and possibly having a mental health crisis.' She wasn't wrong.
script.direction: admitting defeat, self-aware

script.text: At least she was honest.
script.direction: trying to find a silver lining

script.text: She also said she hoped I got the call I was waiting for. So that's where my dating life is right now.
script.direction: rock bottom, but finding it a little funny

script.text: You know what? At least Jenny only controls my gas budget. She doesn't control my entire transportation method.
script.direction: putting his own problems in perspective

script.text: I have a question about this super-liking business. Is it considered more or less forward than asking someone to dance at a church social?
script.direction: genuinely trying to understand modern dating

script.text: Professor, asking someone to dance at a church social is like sending them a handwritten letter compared to super-liking. It's way more classy.
script.direction: explaining the hierarchy, wistful

script.text: Then why don't you simply ask women to dance at church socials?
script.direction: logical follow-up question

script.text: Because I don't go to church and I don't know where they have socials and I'm pretty sure they don't want me there anyway.
script.direction: listing the obvious problems

script.text: Maybe start going to church?
script.direction: helpful suggestion, not really serious

script.text: Leo, I can't even successfully use a dating app. You want me to try organized religion?
script.direction: pointing out his obvious limitations

script.text: Fair point. Let's get you comfortable with basic human interaction first.
script.direction: mock supportive, teasing his friend

script.text: I'm still confused about the technology. If you can see his photograph and he can see yours, why do you need an algorithm to determine compatibility?
script.direction: missing the point entirely, genuinely puzzled

script.text: Because— you know what, I can't explain it because it doesn't make sense. You're right. It's insane that we've outsourced meeting people to our phones.
script.direction: having a moment of clarity, frustrated with himself

script.text: On that surprisingly philosophical note—
script.direction: trying to transition, amused by the turn

script.text: Wait, can we circle back to the Seoul story for a second? That woman getting thirty-three hundred dollars for a home she's lived in for forty years?
script.direction: shifting topics, genuine concern

script.text: It's brutal. The government is basically saying your entire life is worth what most people spend on a vacation.
script.direction: grim assessment

script.text: Urban renewal always sounds noble until you're the one being renewed out of existence.
script.direction: bitter historical perspective

script.text: I keep thinking about my mom. She's been in the same house in Carmel for thirty years. If someone told her it was worth thirty-three hundred dollars she'd laugh and then probably hit them with her purse.
script.direction: personal connection, protective of his mother

script.text: Your mom carries a purse that could be used as a weapon?
script.direction: curious about this detail

script.text: My mom carries a purse that weighs fifteen pounds and contains everything she might possibly need for any conceivable emergency. It's basically a survival kit with a shoulder strap.
script.direction: affectionate description, pride in her preparedness

script.text: What's in there?
script.direction: genuinely curious

script.text: Last time I looked? Band-aids, safety pins, a flashlight, three different kinds of pain relievers, a sewing kit, snacks, and a small hammer.
script.direction: listing items, building to the absurd

script.text: A hammer? In her purse?
script.direction: impressed despite himself

script.text: A small hammer. For emergencies. When I asked what kind of emergencies require a hammer, she said 'You never know, Deepak.'
script.direction: quoting his mother's logic, finding it reasonable

script.text: Your mom sounds like she could survive the apocalypse with the contents of her purse.
script.direction: admiringly

script.text: She absolutely could. And she'd probably reorganize society while she was at it.
script.direction: complete confidence in his mother's abilities

script.text: Alright, before we get too deep into Duke's mom's emergency preparedness, let me hit you with some news that'll make you feel better about your own life choices.
script.direction: transitioning to lighter stories, building anticipation

script.text: Millions of grill brushes have been recalled because they can cause serious internal injuries if the metal bristles get into your food.
script.direction: delivering the absurd news seriously

script.text: Wait, what? How do you not notice metal bristles in your burger?
script.direction: practical concern, as someone who grills

script.text: Apparently they're small enough that you don't notice until they're, you know, perforating your internal organs.
script.direction: grim detail delivered cheerfully

script.text: This is why I clean my grill with a ball of aluminum foil like my father taught me.
script.direction: superiority of old methods

script.text: In other completely normal news, scientists successfully used pig semen as eye drops to deliver cancer treatment to mice.
script.direction: deadpan delivery of the most absurd headline

script.text: I'm sorry, what?
script.direction: sure he misheard

script.text: Pig semen eye drops. For cancer treatment. In mice. I don't understand the science but apparently it worked.
script.direction: equally baffled but committed to reporting it

script.text: Who comes up with these experiments? Like, who was sitting around thinking 'You know what might cure cancer? Pig semen in the eyes.'
script.direction: trying to understand the thought process

script.text: Medical research has always involved unconventional approaches, but this does seem particularly creative.
script.direction: trying to be diplomatic about the weirdness

script.text: The Texas House is considering annexing counties from New Mexico, which feels like the kind of thing that should require more than just considering.
script.direction: moving on to the next absurd story

script.text: That's definitely a federal issue, not a state legislature issue.
script.direction: journalist instincts kicking in

script.text: And finally, someone stole nearly four hundred and fourteen thousand KitKat bars while they were being transported from Italy to Poland.
script.direction: building to the punchline

script.text: Four hundred thousand KitKats? That's not theft, that's a heist.
script.direction: impressed by the scale

script.text: Right? Someone planned this. You don't accidentally steal a truck full of that many chocolate bars.
script.direction: appreciating the criminal ambition

script.text: I have to admire the specificity. Four hundred and fourteen thousand. Someone counted.
script.direction: finding the bureaucratic precision amusing

script.text: Or the manufacturer knows exactly how many were on that truck.
script.direction: practical explanation

script.text: Either way, somewhere in Europe there's someone with a garage full of KitKats trying to figure out how to fence stolen chocolate.
script.direction: enjoying the mental image

script.text: You know what? After hearing about pig semen eye drops, stolen KitKats feels totally normal.
script.direction: putting it in perspective

script.text: That's The Friction for Saturday, March twenty-ninth. I'm Leo Barros.
script.direction: wrapping up, professional sign-off

script.text: Dr. Haywood Pringle. I shall be researching what a thirst trap is, apparently.
script.direction: resigned to learning about modern culture

script.text: Duke Patel. Jenny says I'm allowed to mention her on the podcast but she's not taking responsibility for any of my stories.
script.direction: reporting his wife's position

script.text: And I'm Jax Mooney, still taking the bus and still matched with my former Uber driver. If anyone has suggestions for how to handle this situation, please send help. See you Monday.
script.direction: callback to his dating disaster, still seeking solutions
